title: Uni grants casuals historic super deal
full_text:
The University of Newcastle has agreed to pay 17 per cent superannuation to casual staff from 2030, in a precedent-setting deal for the tertiary sector.
The National Tertiary Education Union said the agreement, the first struck by a university, would have a “seismic effect” across the sector, and the union would push for similar entitlements at other universities.
Permanent university staff currently receive 17 per cent superannuation but casuals are paid the legal minimum 12 per cent rate.
NTEU general secretary Damien Cahill said universities’ “addiction to casualisation” was creating a retirement savings chasm that put already precariously employed staff under huge pressure later in life. “This is the first domino to fall and it won’t be the last. We will continue to campaign to ensure all universities pay casual staff the 17 per cent superannuation they deserve,” he said.
Dr Cahill said if universities were serious about rebuilding trust with the public, ending two-tier superannuation was a solid place to start. “This has been more than a decade in the making. The implications for higher education are enormous,” he said.
“Workers and employers right across the country should be taking their lead from NTEU members and making this an economy-wide entitlement for all casuals.” The increased superannuation for casual employees followed 12 days of conferences before the Fair Work Commission between the university, the NTEU and the Community and Public Sector Union that resulted in new in-principle enterprise agreements proposing 15.2 per cent in pay rises by 2029.
The 17 per cent superannuation claim for casuals was the only outstanding issue. While the commission said the claim had merit, it said “no other university in the sector provides such benefits to casual employees”, recommending both unions pursue the claim as a sector-wide issue in the next round of bargaining. However, at a follow-up meeting, agreement was reached to introduce the entitlement from 2030.
In a note to staff, University of Newcastle vice-chancellor and president Alex Zelinsky said he understood and recognised that superannuation arrangements for casuals was an “important issue for our staff and for the sector more broadly”.
“This commitment represents an important step toward improving superannuation equity for casual staff,” Professor Zelinsky said.
The NTEU originally won 17 per cent super for continuing staff in 1993, and has been at the forefront of industrial gains such as paid parental leave, recognition of same-sex partnerships and paid domestic violence leave.
NTEU University of Newcastle branch president Terry Summers said the university’s NTEU members should be “incredibly proud of being the first in the country to secure this historic entitlement”.
“We didn’t win this fight overnight – it was the product of a hard campaign across many years of bargaining,” Dr Summers said.
“What we’ve won in Newcastle will have seismic effects right across Australia. “The days of casuals being dudded on super are numbered. “Combined with superannuation equity for periods of unpaid parental leave, UON staff will now enjoy the best superannuation entitlements of any Australian university.”
summary: The University of Newcastle has agreed to provide 17% superannuation for casual staff starting in 2030, setting a precedent in the tertiary sector.

heading: Superannuation and Investments
key_points: This is the first university agreement to offer 17% superannuation to casual staff.
The National Tertiary Education Union aims to extend this entitlement across other universities.
The agreement follows negotiations that also proposed significant pay rises for casual staff.
relevance: This development may influence superannuation policies and employment practices within the financial services sector, particularly regarding casual workers.
